design_philosophy.core_loop: Navigate side-scrolling platformer levels while avoiding obstacles and enemies; collect screwdrivers to reattach lost limbs before losing a life.
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Limb loss system creates escalating penalty for enemy/hazard contact; players must manage cumulative damage before catastrophic head loss.
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Playing as Slick, a crash test dummy, rescuing Dr. Zub from Junkman in a cartoon action adventure. Licensed toy tie-in with mechanical character premise.
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Unconventional damage model (limb loss rather than health bar) creates distinct resource management tension. Designed primarily for younger players with reduced mechanical sophistication, trading depth for toy-line narrative appeal.

difficulty.lives_system: Multi-hit system: lose individual limbs on hazard contact, lose life when head falls off after all limbs lost.
difficulty.checkpointing: Divided into five areas with midlevel bosses per area; checkpointing structure implied but not explicitly documented.
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Hidden level select (L, R, B, L, R, A, L, R, X at title screen) allows player bypass of intended progression sequence.

progression.upgrade_system: Screwdriver pickups serve as limb restoration items rather than permanent upgrades.
progression.unlock_mechanism: Five sequential areas with area bosses (Junkbots) guarding progression to final boss (Junkman).
replayability_hooks: Hidden level select enables non-linear play
Toy line tie-in content may appeal to series fans
Mixed reception suggests lower replay incentive for general audiences

world_building_techniques: Licensed property from 1993 FOX Kids animated TV special
Character-driven rescue narrative (Slick rescues Dr. Zub from Junkman)
Mechanical dummy premise justifies limb-loss damage system thematically

boss_design.philosophy: Area guardians (Junkbots) serve midlevel roles; Junkman functions as final, presumably more complex encounter. Boss placement tied to area-based progression structure.

danger_signals: Progressive damage accumulation toward head loss/game over
environmental_storytelling: Five distinct areas suggesting Junkman's domain/lair structure
Junkyard aesthetic implied by naming conventions
Mechanical toy characters in industrial setting

sources_used: Wikipedia: The Incredible Crash Dummies (video game) - gameplay sections for Amiga/Genesis/SNES and NES versions
The Cutting Room Floor: The Incredible Crash Dummies (SNES) - developer/publisher credits and hidden level select documentation
